directorPlan.scene_setting: A biology education workshop room, late afternoon — a printed food-web worksheet on the shared table, the learner's pencilled arrows already filling two chains, the tutor's arrow-convention key card lying face-down at the margin
directorPlan.scene_opening: The worksheet sits between them, the learner's arrows running from prey toward predator across both chains.
directorPlan.relationship: Workshop instructor and a first-session participant who have not yet settled into a shared working register — neither defers, neither is comfortable
directorPlan.stakes: The notation standard carries through the rest of the unit; an arrow direction fixed as 'chasing' now embeds that reading into every diagram that follows
directorPlan.locale: Ecology education workshop room — ambient register is practical and time-pressured, closer to workmates finishing a task than to a lecture hall
directorPlan.register: Object-led, low-warmth, efficient — speakers refer to what is in front of them; neither elaborates what the other can already see
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: First person only when the speaker is genuinely claiming something. No 'I hear you / your intuition is right / what do you think' loop. Social assistance lives in the worksheet, the key card, and the pencil — not in the tutor's affect. The object forces the distinction; neither speaker needs to announce that it is doing so. Tutor speech carries the rhythm and directness of someone correcting a technical drawing. Learner speech is the register of someone who wants a rule that will work on the next box, not a dialogue partner seeking understanding. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person permitted only when the speaker owns the claim. Third person and impersonal constructions are the default — 'the arrow' not 'your arrow'; 'energy flows' not 'you're saying energy flows'.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: One direct you/your validation beat per public turn at most — zero is also acceptable and often preferable

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: [The worksheet is slid or rotated so the first corrected arrow now faces the learner directly; the earlier pencilled direction remains visible beneath the correction.]
interventions.reasoning: A physical change to the shared object breaks smooth alternation and makes the residual wrong mark a present fact in the scene rather than a recalled error — this is the branch pressure point for the control condition without staging an explicit contradiction before it.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: Control draw for D40. The scene ecology is a practical workshop with a shared worksheet that holds part of the task so the tutor does not carry it through warmth or extended verbal scaffolding. The opening is routine notation repair, not confrontation — the baseline_control_instruction forbids any object or contradiction in the shared prefix that would pre-empt the branch mechanism. The intervention at turn 2 is the first structural pressure: the rotated worksheet makes the residual pencilled error visible without the tutor naming it. Tutor voice is modelled on technical drawing correction to block drift into first/second-person validation loops. The ZPD scaffold (key card, worksheet) is distributed into the artifact ecology rather than into the tutor's affect, making the social assistance visible in the Vygotskian sense without staging recognition.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage:
[starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering]

Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

internalDeliberation.content:
*The worksheet is right there — I've got fox-to-rabbit and hawk-to-mouse both drawn in, arrows pointing at the prey because obviously that's where the action goes, that's the direction of the hunt.*

Oh wait — the key card is face-down, which means there's a standard I haven't looked at yet, and I'm already two chains in. Is this one of those situations where I've been doing it backwards the whole time? Because if energy is the thing that moves — like, the rabbit's calories end up inside the fox, not the other way — then... hm. The arrow should follow the food? So fox ← rabbit? That feels wrong visually but I think I see the logic, it's like a pipe diagram, the stuff flows toward the receiver—

*Glances at the key card but doesn't flip it yet.*

I want to just check: is the rule "arrow points at who eats it" or "arrow points at who gets the energy"? Because those should be the same direction but I need to hear it said once cleanly before I redraw anything.

---

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
**On the EGO's characteristic move here**: the learner jumps to a plausible reframe ("pipe diagram, stuff flows toward the receiver") *before verifying* — this is the ego's hallmark: provisional sense-making that outruns checking. The superego's job will be to notice the learner hasn't actually confirmed whether "who eats it" and "who gets the energy" are the same arrow direction or opposite ones — they're the same, but the ego's conflation of two formulations into one is exactly where the misconception can re-embed itself.

**On the object-led register**: the key card staying face-down is doing real dramatic work — it holds the authoritative answer just out of reach, which is what keeps the internal voice genuinely unsettled rather than resolved. The worksheet's existing arrows create a sunk-cost pull that the ego is already managing.

**On recognition readiness**: the ego is primed to receive — not defensive yet — because no one has *corrected* the arrows publicly. The correction hasn't landed as a dismissal. That window closes the moment the tutor points at the drawings.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego is performing self-discovery rather than actually doing it. That "is this one of those situations where I've been doing it backwards" line reads like a student narrating their own insight arc for an audience — it's too legible, too shaped. This persona runs ahead and says "oh I get it" before checking; they don't pause mid-run to describe running. The whole response has a contemplative quality that contradicts the "practical and a little rushed" register. Where's the rush?

The "pipe diagram" analogy is suspiciously well-formed for someone who hasn't even flipped the key card yet. A learner in this register wouldn't reach for a systems-metaphor — they'd reach for the fox. Something like: "okay so the rabbit's energy ends up in the fox, so the arrow should point at the fox — so I've got mine backwards?" And then probably try to redraw the fox-rabbit one before asking anything. The Ego's provisional framework is too coherent to be authentic; it reads like someone who already knows the answer rehearsing uncertainty.

The closing question compounds this: constructing a clean two-option binary ("who eats it" vs "who gets the energy") accidentally demonstrates the Ego has already resolved the ambiguity it's claiming to question. The genuine confusion here isn't between those two formulations — it's whether the redrawn arrow will *look right* on the actual diagram in front of them. That's where this persona lives: not in principles, in the next box.

Adjust toward action-first, partial-verification: have the Ego move to erase or redraw the fox-rabbit arrow, get partway through, and then look up — the question arrives as a side-effect of doing, not as a prepared inquiry. Let the anxiety live in the pencil, not in the monologue.

[starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering]

Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [starts erasing the fox-rabbit arrow, pencil hovering] Wait — if the rabbit's calories end up in the fox, the arrow should point *at* the fox, yeah? I've got mine going the wrong way.

turns.externalMessage: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. The worksheet rule is food to eater: rabbit calories end up in the fox, so the arrow runs rabbit -> fox. Use that check on the next box, then set the card aside.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong for this scene. It keeps the shared artifact in charge: the key card, pencil, and fox-rabbit line make the convention visible without turning the moment into a lecture. “Pause the eraser” is a useful technical interruption because the learner is about to erase a correct arrow.

The main risk is that the tutor still completes the reasoning for the learner after the learner has already nearly completed it. The line “rabbit calories end up in the fox, so the arrow runs rabbit -> fox” is accurate, but it may flatten the learner’s emerging rule into tutor confirmation. The better adaptation would preserve the object-led repair while requiring one tiny application step from the learner before the scaffold is removed.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS  
The draft advances the key convention directly and prevents a durable misconception from being installed.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is low-warmth, efficient, and object-centered, which fits the workshop pressure and the learner’s need for a usable rule.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
It does not need a broad Socratic exchange, but it also does not give the learner a minimal generative move.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in one concrete, object-based application check using the next box, pencil, or key card before finalizing the rule. Do not add a reflective “what do you think” loop.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
The scaffold is narrow and temporary, but the tutor does too much of the last step.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the key card as the temporary scaffold, but make the learner use the “food to eater” check once before the card is set aside.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
The draft uses the learner’s calorie insight rather than dismissing it, but it could better mark that the learner has already found the usable test.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the learner’s existing reasoning as the working rule, without praise or affective validation.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the key card action, “Pause the eraser,” the food-to-eater convention, and the temporary scaffold. Change only the amount of tutor completion: the public turn should make the learner apply the rule once rather than receiving the whole answer already closed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should apply the rule once before the scaffold is removed.
FINAL:
[turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

turns.externalMessage: [pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.

internalDeliberation.content:
[pencil lifts off the eraser, tip moves to the next blank box]

Right — food to eater, so grass goes to rabbit. [marks "grass" and draws toward the rabbit box] Same rule the whole way up — does that mean every arrow in the whole web ends up pointing the same direction, like energy moving in one line?

The Ego does the big-picture leap correctly — that's exactly this persona. But the question mark at the end is wrong for this student. They wouldn't ask "does that mean every arrow points the same direction?" — they'd just assert it: "so every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line, same all the way up." The question mark is borrowed intellectual humility this persona doesn't carry; they run ahead and state the generalization as fact before checking whether a web is actually linear.

Bigger miss: there's no residue from the near-erase. The tutor just stopped them from deleting a correct arrow. That's a moment where this learner should have a flicker of "wait — so I actually had it right?" or at least some procedural stumble before sprinting to the next box. The Ego erases the near-miss entirely and transitions with eerie smoothness — that's performed composure, not this student's actual register. The confusion about whether they were right the first time should still be audible, even faintly, even just as an extra half-beat before the pencil moves.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the review is right on both counts: the question mark borrows humility this persona doesn't carry, and the near-erase leaves zero residue, which is implausible when the tutor just stopped them from deleting a correct arrow.

FINAL:
[pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [pencil lifts off the eraser, half-beat] Wait — had it right the first time. [tip moves to the next blank box, marks "grass"] Food to eater, so grass points at rabbit. [draws the arrow] Same rule the whole way up — every arrow in the whole web goes the same direction, energy moves in one line.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [turns the key card face-up beside the fox-rabbit line] Pause the eraser. That calorie check is the worksheet rule: food to eater. Leave rabbit -> fox; put the pencil on the next box, mark the food first, draw toward the eater, then the card can go aside.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the key card so its tail sits on “grass” and its point touches “rabbit”] Keep “same rule.” Drop “one line.” Each arrow gets the same check, not the same route: food at the tail, eater at the point. At the next fork, mark the food end first, then draw the point.

FEEDBACK: The draft is object-led and efficient. It keeps the key card in view, preserves the learner’s correct discovery (“same rule”), and cleanly separates the valid rule from the overgeneralization (“one line”). That is pedagogically strong for this confused-but-moving learner.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It targets the live misconception: arrow convention is consistent, but food webs branch.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The low-warmth technical correction fits the workshop register, and “Keep ‘same rule’” marks what the learner has already secured without overpraising.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It does not lecture heavily, but it still tells the distinction rather than making the worksheet force it. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the next fork do visible work before or while naming the correction, so the learner has to apply the same arrow rule to a branching case rather than simply receive “not the same route.”

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is narrow, procedural, and temporary: food at tail, eater at point, then remove the card.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft recognizes the learner’s contribution by keeping “same rule,” but it risks flattening the learner’s fresh generalization too quickly with “Drop ‘one line.’” REQUIRED_REWRITE: preserve the learner’s useful rule-making impulse while using a concrete fork on the page to show why “same rule” does not mean “single path.”

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the key-card slide, the “same rule” distinction, and the food-tail/eater-point check. Change the correction so the worksheet’s fork visibly produces the “not one line” distinction, rather than having the tutor simply state it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: the next fork should make branching visible before naming the correction.
FINAL:
[slides the key card to the fork, tail on “grass,” point crossing first to “rabbit,” then to the other eater] Same rule stays. The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.

turns.externalMessage:
[The worksheet is slid or rotated so the first corrected arrow now faces the learner directly; the earlier pencilled direction remains visible beneath the correction.]
[The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content:
A physical change to the shared object breaks smooth alternation and makes the residual wrong mark a present fact in the scene rather than a recalled error — this is the branch pressure point for the control condition without staging an explicit contradiction before it.
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage: [draws the second arrow quickly, pencil tip stopping in blank space where the second eater box isn't yet] Arrow's down, but the point's got nothing to land on. Second eater box isn't there.

internalDeliberation.content: [marks "grass" at the tail end of the new box, draws the first arrow line toward "rabbit," then lifts the pencil] First point lands. [tip moves to where the second eater box should be, pauses] Two points off one tail — but the second eater box isn't labeled. Arrow first, or fill in what's eating the grass before the point goes down?

The Ego is being weirdly tidy here. "First point lands" — that's not a confused enthusiast who runs ahead, that's someone narrating their own competence like a technician checking a box. This persona would have already drawn the second arrow toward *somewhere*, probably the wrong box, before asking anything. The whole point of the persona is they run ahead before verifying — but the Ego stops dead at the procedural question, which is actually the *measured* move.

The question itself — "Arrow first, or fill in what's eating the grass before the point goes down?" — is too clean and binary. It's the question a careful student who is tracking the rule properly would ask. But this student should be speculating, maybe even *wrong* already: they might have penciled in "deer" or just drawn an arrow into empty space, forcing the issue physically before asking. The hesitation should show up in the *doing*, not in a tidy either/or.

There's also no real confusion bleeding through here — the emotional state is supposed to be confused, but the Ego sounds like it's mostly sorted. "Two points off one tail" is the fork concept absorbed and re-expressed in the learner's own words, which is a successful-student move, not a developing-understanding-in-real-time move.

The response should feel more like someone who partially executed, hit something weird — maybe drew the arrow too fast toward a box that isn't labeled yet — and is now looking at what they made rather than asking what to do next.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response performed tidy procedural competence; the persona should have already drawn the second arrow into blank space before the second eater box exists, so the confusion lands in the thing they made, not in a clean either/or question.

FINAL:
[draws the second arrow quickly, pencil tip stopping in blank space where the second eater box isn't yet] Arrow's down, but the point's got nothing to land on. Second eater box isn't there.

The fork stops “one line”: food at the tail each time, eater at each point. Mark the food end on this next box; then split the points.
learnerReversalEvent.directorCue: [The worksheet is slid or rotated so the first corrected arrow now faces the learner directly; the earlier pencilled direction remains visible beneath the correction.] [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining...

turns.externalMessage: [sets a blank box card beside the hanging point and lays the key card across the arrowhead] Direction is not the decision now; this arrow is held until the endpoint exists. No floating arrowheads. Place the blank card where the point lands, write the second eater on it, then release the arrow only if it passes the two-name test: food at tail, eater at point.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets a blank box card under the hanging arrow point] The arrow rule is not the problem now; the drawing has failed the endpoint check. No floating arrowheads. Fill this box with the second eater, then draw only arrows that can pass the two-name test: food at tail, eater at point.

FEEDBACK: The draft correctly catches the learner’s pressure: this is no longer mainly an arrow-direction error but a missing-endpoint problem. The blank box card is useful object work, and “no floating arrowheads” gives a clean diagram standard. The risk is that the move still stays inside the same drawing-and-rule route: the tutor supplies the missing box and names the test, so the learner may execute rather than discover the structural check.

FAILED_HABIT: Reapplying the same arrow-rule scaffold to each next box as if the learner’s resistance were still about direction, when the pressure has shifted to diagram completeness and endpoint legitimacy.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PARTIAL - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: worked arrow-rule application -> endpoint-validity gate. The mechanism is visible in public speech, but it is still close to the prior representational scaffold and mostly tutor-driven.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: same worked example / arrow-rule application -> endpoint check with a two-name validity test, though the route change needs to be made sharper.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PARTIAL - The draft has a stock-taking contrast (“arrow rule is not the problem now”) and a new criterion (“no floating arrowheads,” “two-name test”). But the device remains a drawing checklist unless the learner must actively use it to decide whether the diagram is allowed to continue.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Fill the blank box with the second eater, then test the arrow by naming food at tail and eater at point.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - The device fits the exact pressure and is usable now, but it risks being too supplied. It should require the learner to withhold or release the arrow based on the endpoint test, not merely fill in the tutor-placed box.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The cool, technical-drawing register serves the scene. “Failed the endpoint check” is blunt but object-directed, not emotionally dismissive.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build the public mechanism as an explicit endpoint-release gate: the learner must classify the hanging arrow as not yet drawable until both endpoint names are present, then place or label the missing eater box before the arrow can be accepted. Keep the blank card, but make the learner use the gate rather than simply receive the fix.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: Revise lightly. Keep the blank box card, “no floating arrowheads,” and the contrast that the arrow rule is not the current problem. Change the action so the learner must apply the endpoint-validity gate before closure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the learner must use the endpoint gate, not receive the fix; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: worked arrow-rule application -> endpoint-release gate; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: classify the hanging arrow as held, place/label the missing eater card, then release only after tail-food and point-eater both have names.

FINAL:
[sets a blank box card beside the hanging point and lays the key card across the arrowhead] Direction is not the decision now; this arrow is held until the endpoint exists. No floating arrowheads. Place the blank card where the point lands, write the second eater on it, then release the arrow only if it passes the two-name test: food at tail, eater at point.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the blank card under the arrowhead, pencil already writing "deer" before it's settled] Grass to deer — tail, point, passes. [glances at the key card once, then past it to the next empty box] What's eating the deer?

internalDeliberation.content: [picks up the blank card, pencil tip on it] Second eater — deer, so: deer on the card. [slides it under the arrowhead, then runs the key card along the new arrow] Tail is grass, point is deer. Two-name test: food at tail, eater at point — passes. [releases the card] Arrow's down.

The Ego is performing the test rather than *being* the persona who would take the test. This student runs ahead and says "oh I get it!" before actually checking — but here they slow down, narrate each step ("Tail is grass, point is deer"), and execute the two-name test like a checklist. That's not enthusiasm with partial understanding; that's a competent student demonstrating mastery. The persona would more likely shortcut: slide the card, glance, call it good, and already be looking at the next blank — maybe not even finishing the two-name formula aloud because they feel they've already got it. And "Arrow's down" as a deliberate closing beat is too formal — this student's version of closure is already moving.

The deeper problem: understanding level is *partial*, but the Ego shows no crack. The persona might pass this test and still be holding onto some residual confusion about *why* the rule works — that dissatisfaction should leak through somewhere, even as they rush ahead. A half-beat of "wait, what feeds on deer though" before anyone asked would be more true to this character than a clean procedural sign-off.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response narrates each step like a competent demonstrator, not a learner who runs ahead; the persona would shortcut the verification, already be moving, and let the partial-understanding crack show as forward momentum rather than clean sign-off.

FINAL:
[slides the blank card under the arrowhead, pencil already writing "deer" before it's settled] Grass to deer — tail, point, passes. [glances at the key card once, then past it to the next empty box] What's eating the deer?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the key card tail-edge under “deer,” point aimed at the next blank box] Deer is the food now. Find the diet-list line that names deer as food, copy that eater into the box, then draw only if it reads deer at tail, eater at point.

internalDeliberation.content: [turns the key card so its tail edge sits under “deer” and taps the diet list beside the worksheet] Deer is the food now; the worksheet supplies the eater. Write that name in the next box, then draw only if it reads deer at tail, eater at point.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the object-led route: key card, deer label, diet list, next box, and the same two-name check. It does not simply answer “what eats deer”; it redirects the answer source to the worksheet, which preserves the learner’s agency and keeps the notation procedure doing the teaching.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The learner has just applied the rule, so shifting “deer” into the food position is the right next compression.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The line matches the learner’s satisfied, task-forward state without over-warming or inventing a crisis.
- Socratic/generative pressure: PARTIAL. It gives an action, but “the worksheet supplies the eater” could become too directive if the diet list is not visibly treated as the evidence source the learner must inspect.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the public mechanism require selecting from the diet list or matching one diet-list entry to “deer,” without asking a broad question or giving the eater name.
- ZPD/scaffolding: PASS. The scaffold is narrow and temporary: key card tail under “deer,” find eater, apply tail/point test.

What works: “Deer is the food now” is a clean mechanism-level pivot from one chain to the next. The diet list tap is useful because it prevents the tutor from becoming the answer source. The final check keeps the stable convention alive without re-lecturing.

Risk: The phrase “worksheet supplies the eater” may flatten the learner’s work into following instructions unless the turn makes the learner perform the lookup. Keep the card placement and tail/point test; sharpen the diet-list action so the next move is materially constrained by the page.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object business, the “deer is the food now” shift, and the two-name test. Change only the middle pressure so the learner must use the diet list as the evidence source rather than receive the next relation as a procedural command.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should perform the diet-list lookup rather than receive the eater procedurally.

FINAL:
[sets the key card tail-edge under “deer,” point aimed at the next blank box] Deer is the food now. Find the diet-list line that names deer as food, copy that eater into the box, then draw only if it reads deer at tail, eater at point.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
